messages.content:
Write a well-structured research report of 4-5 paragraphs that answers the question below using ONLY the evidence provided. Requirements:
  - Clear structure (intro, themed body paragraphs, brief conclusion).
  - Cite sources inline by their URL where claims are made.
  - Be temporally accurate (mention relevant dates/years).
  - Do not invent facts beyond the evidence.

Research question: How has the implementation of the UK's post-Brexit immigration policy affected its labor market and key industries between 2021-2024? Include specific policy changes and their measured impacts.

Changes to the points-based system and the Skilled Worker visa programme has opened up opportunities for non-EU workers, leading to a surge in the interest of overseas talent for UK jobs. This article delves into the implications of these changes for UK businesses, the process of obtaining a sponsor licence , and the costs involved. 1. Title: Addressing labour shortages in the hospitality sector
Snippet: UK hospitality sector faces labour shortages due to immigration changes and rising costs, requiring strategic recruitment and retention.
Content: Addressing labour shortages in the hospitality sector â Moore Kingston Smith Addressing labour shortages in the hospitality sector 5 December 2024 / Insight posted in Articles The UKâs hospitality sector has been grappling with significant labour shortages, a challenge exacerbated by recent changes to UK immigration policy. These shortages have led to increased pressure on existing staff, longer working hours and a decline in service quality. As a result, businesses are struggling to recruit and retain staff , leading to higher turnover rates and increased operational costs. Addressing these challenges requires a multi-faceted approach, including improving working conditions, offering competitive wages and exploring new recruitment strategies both domestically and internationally. Current landscape The hospitality industry in the UK has traditionally relied on an international workforce. However, recent adjustments to the immigration system have further constrained the talent pool. Key changes include: Brexit: The UKâs departure from the European Union has had a profound impact on the hospitality sector. Prior to Brexit, EU nationals accounted for a significant portion of the UKâs

Snippet: ... figure represents the sharpest recorded decline in UK net migration in the modern data series, driven by a combination of visa policy tightening under ...
Content: UK British Immigration Statistics 2026 | Numbers, Destinations & Facts â The Global Statistics Skip to content Post category: Population UK Immigration in 2026 UK immigration in 2026 is at a pivotal inflection point â one defined by a dramatic statistical reversal, deepening political controversy, and the long-term structural consequences of decisions made between 2021 and 2023. The headline number from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) tells a striking story: net migration to the UK in the year to June 2025 was 204,000 â a 69% fall from 649,000 in the previous year , and the lowest annual figure since 2021. That single figure represents the sharpest recorded decline in UK net migration in the modern data series, driven by a combination of visa policy tightening under both the outgoing Conservative government and the Labour government that took office in July 2024 , falling demand for UK study visas following the January 2024 dependants ban , and a sharp rise in emigration. To put the scale of the reversal in context: UK net migration peaked at a historical record of 906,000 in the year to June 2023 â and has since contracted by over 77% in just two years. Report:

response:
# The Impact of UK Post-Brexit Immigration Policy on Labor Markets and Industries (2021-2024)

The United Kingdom's departure from the European Union fundamentally transformed its immigration landscape, replacing free movement with a new points-based system that has produced dramatic shifts in both migration patterns and labor market outcomes. Following Brexit, the UK implemented a points-based immigration system and expanded its Skilled Worker visa programme, which "made the UK an attractive destination for overseas workers, particularly from non-EU countries" (https://workpermit.com/news/post-brexit-uk-visa-doors-open-non-eu-workers-20230904). This represented a complete departure from the pre-Brexit framework, as the end of free movement necessitated comprehensive changes to UK immigration law and visa policy (https://www.visaverge.com/questions/post-brexit-immigration-changes-updates-to-uk-immigration-law-and-visa-policy/).

The policy changes produced unprecedented volatility in migration numbers between 2021 and 2024. Net migration to the UK reached a historical record of 906,000 in the year to June 2023, driven largely by increased non-EU immigration (https://www.theglobalstatistics.com/uk-british-immigration-statistics/). The Office for National Statistics confirmed that net migration hit an all-time high in 2022, with non-EU immigration becoming the dominant source (https://workpermit.com/news/record-net-migration-uk-tory-brexit-20230525). However, this surge proved temporary. By June 2025, net migration had plummeted to 204,000âa 69% fall from 649,000 the previous year and a 77% contraction from the 2023 peak. This dramatic reversal resulted from "visa policy tightening under both the outgoing Conservative government and the Labour government that took office in July 2024," including a January 2024 ban on dependants for study visa holders (https://www.theglobalstatistics.com/uk-british-immigration-statistics/).

Specific industries experienced severe labor shortages as a direct consequence of Brexit-related immigration restrictions. The hospitality sector faced particularly acute challenges, having "traditionally relied on an international workforce" that was constrained by "recent adjustments to the immigration system" following the UK's departure from the European Union, when "EU nationals accounted for a significant portion of the UK's" hospitality workforce (https://mooreks.co.uk/insights/addressing-labour-shortages-in-the-hospitality-sector/). In September 2021, industry experts warned that "the pandemic bears some responsibility for the UK's labour shortage but a big share of the blame has to go to Brexit," characterizing it as "not a short term issue" that "underlines the skills challenge that has faced the sector for many years" (https://northeastpost.co.uk/article/UK-Labour-Shortage-Brexit-Is-Just-As-Much-To-Blame-As-The-Pandemic). The crisis became so severe that by August 2021, the UK faced the prospect of culling 70,000 healthy pigs due to labor shortages, with industry groups proposing changes to allow workers to enter "on a one-year visa" (https://newseu.cgtn.com/news/2021-08-31/Menus-minus-milkshakes-and-meat-now-a-pig-cull-UK-labor-shortage-139LjcfwxoY/index.html).

The measured impacts reveal a complex picture where initial predictions about trade effects proved accurate, but immigration outcomes diverged significantly from expectations. Research indicated that "the impact on trade overall appears to have been broadly consistent with predictions so far, that on immigration much less negative (and perhaps" more positive than anticipated) (https://cepr.org/voxeu/columns/impact-brexit-uk-economy-reviewing-evidence). The first half of 2024 saw continued policy adjustments, with the Migration Advisory Committee documenting rule changes affecting various professional categories, including approximately 7,000 visas issued to Natural and Social Science professionals, primarily within the education sector (https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/migration-advisory-committee-annual-report-2024/migration-advisory-committee-mac-annual-report-2024-accessible). These shifts demonstrate how post-Brexit immigration policy created a period of extreme fluctuationâfrom acute labor shortages in 2021-2022 to record migration in 2023, followed by sharp contraction in 2024âfundamentally reshaping the UK's workforce composition and creating ongoing challenges for industries dependent on international labor.
